data.title: Swedish Construction Output Rebounds in March
data.description:
Construction activity in Sweden expanded by 2.2% year-on-year in March 2025, recovering from an upwardly revised 1.3% drop in the previous month.

This marks the first growth in construction output in three months, with the sector accounting for 9.1% of the total value in the private sector.

Compared to the prior month, construction activity grew by a seasonally adjusted 1.0% in March.

data.title: Italy's Enel Confirms 2025 Outlook, Share Buyback Plans After 'Solid' First Quarter
data.description:
Enel (ENEL.MI) affirmed its financial targets for 2025 and reiterated plans to launch a share repurchase program after delivering a "solid" first-quarter performance.

The Italian utility giant on Thursday reported an attributable profit of 2.01 billion euros for the three months ended March 31, against 1.93 billion euros a year ago. Total revenue also rose to 22.07 billion euros from 19.43 billion euros.

The group's profit results reflect the "positive" developments in its ordinary operations, with a further boost from lower financial expenses amid a decline in net financial debt. As of March 31, the company's net financial debt stood at 56.01 billion euros, with the net debt-to-equity ratio at 1.06.

On the other hand, Enel's revenue growth reflects the rise in its electricity production and distribution, and the impact of consolidation changes in the two comparable periods, mainly related to the sale of its assets in Peru. The company also benefited from higher revenue in its thermal generation and trading business area due to the sale of commodities on the wholesale market, driven by an annual increase in average prices.

As such, Enel continues to target 6.7 billion euros to 6.9 billion euros in net ordinary income for full-year 2025.

"Despite the reduction in end customer prices in Italy, we confirm for the seventh consecutive quarter the organic and sustainable growth path communicated to the markets, thanks to the optimization of processes, activities and products alongside the contribution of Iberia and the Americas," Enel Chief Executive Officer Flavio Cattaneo said.

During the earnings call with analysts, Chief Financial Officer Stefano De Angelis said the management will propose the 3.5 billion-euro share buyback program, previously mentioned in March, at the annual general meeting on May 22. De Angelis called the move "one of the actionable value creation lever" of the company.

data.title: Travis Perkins Appoints SIG Chief Gavin Slark as CEO
data.description:
(06:13 GMT) *Travis Perkins Appoints Gavin Slark as Its Next CEO
(06:14 GMT) *Travis Perkins: Slark to Become CEO No Later Than Jan 1
(06:24 GMT) Travis Perkins Appoints SIG Chief Gavin Slark as CEO

By Michael Susin

Travis Perkins said it appointed Gavin Slark as chief executive officer, effective by no later than Jan. 1.

The U.K. building-materials retailer on Friday said that Slark served as CEO of SIG since 2023, prior to which he was CEO of Grafton Group for over 10 years, bringing significant experience of the building materials and merchanting industry.

Slark was also CEO of BSS Group, prior to its acquisition by Travis Perkins.

Write to Michael Susin at michael.susin@wsj.com

data.title: Stocks jump in Japan, Taiwan on trade optimism; bitcoin soars
data.description:
Key points:
  • U.S. deal with UK is first since Trump's tariff pause
  • U.S. officials to meet with Chinese negotiators on Saturday
  • Bitcoin leaps to highest since January; gold falls for third day
  • Chip shares rise in Japan, Taiwan; China stocks struggle

Stocks surged in Japan and Taiwan on Friday after a U.S. trade deal with Britain fuelled guarded optimism for progress in tariff talks with other countries.

Bitcoin soared to the highest since January and U.S. crude ticked up after a more than 3% surge on Thursday, when President Donald Trump announced the agreement with British Prime Minister Keir Starmer - the first in the month since Trump started a 90-day pause on trade tariffs to allow room for negotiations.

At the same time, concerns that the limited trade agreement with London may not provide much of a blueprint for additional deals cooled hopes around the outcome of Sino-U.S. trade talks set for Saturday in Switzerland.

Mainland blue chips eased 0.1%, while Hong Kong's Hang Seng rose 0.3% after struggling for direction during the session.

Japan's Nikkei soared 1.7% and Taiwan's equity benchmark TWSE:TAIEX advanced 1.8%, with technology shares the strongest performing sector.

Australian stocks rose 0.5% and Singapore's Straits Times Index added 0.8%.

MSCI's broadest index of Asia-Pacific shares outside Japan (.MIAPJ0000PUS) gained 0.4%.

"The deal between the U.S. and UK was more style over substance," said Kyle Rodda, a senior financial markets analyst at Capital.com.

"However, it feeds the narrative that the U.S. is looking to bang out rapid-fire trade deals and reduce tariffs - at the margins - and other trade barriers," Rodda said.

"Constructive language and statements of intent will likely be enough to drive stocks higher off the back of the U.S.-China trade talks."

Trump pushed back against seeing the UK deal as a template for other negotiations.

The "general terms" agreement leaves in place a 10% tariff on goods imported from the UK but lowers prohibitive U.S. duties on UK car exports. Britain agreed to lower its tariffs to 1.8% from 5.1% and provide greater access to U.S. goods.

Last week, Trump said he has "potential" trade deals with India, South Korea and Japan.

NYMEX U.S. crude ticked up 0.3% to $60.25 per barrel on Friday, building on the previous day's 3.2% surge. Brent crude added 0.3% to $63.18 per barrel, following Thursday's 2.8% rally.

The U.S. dollar index , which measures the currency against six major peers, edged to a one-month peak at 100.86.

The euro sagged to a one-month trough at $1.1197, and sterling slipped to a three-week low of $1.3213.

The yen dipped to a one-month low of 146.185 per dollar, after a 1.5% tumble on Thursday.

Higher U.S. Treasury yields helped support the greenback, with the 10-year yield steady at 4.3667% following Thursday's 10-basis point jump as demand for the safety of bonds ebbed.

Bitcoin was also buoyed by the improvement in market sentiment, rising to the highest since January 31 at $103,909.17, and closing the distance with the all-time high from January 20 at $109,071.86.

Standard Chartered's Geoffrey Kendrick no longer sees risk sentiment as the main driver for the world's biggest cryptocurrency.

"It is now all about flows, and flows are coming in many forms," said Kendrick, the bank's global head of digital assets research, pointing to an influx of cash into bitcoin ETFs, as well as buying by so-called whales.

"I think a fresh all-time high for bitcoin is coming soon," he said. "I apologise that my $120,000 Q2 target may be too low."

data.title: Cellnex Net Loss Widens on Layoff Costs
data.description:
(06:02 GMT) *Cellnex 1Q Rev EUR964M
(06:03 GMT) *Cellnex 1Q Adjusted Ebitda EUR798M
(06:06 GMT) *Cellnex 1Q Oper Pft EUR115M
(06:06 GMT) *Cellnex 1Q Net Loss EUR49M
(06:07 GMT) *Cellnex Backs 2025 View
(06:07 GMT) *Cellnex Declares Dividend of EUR0.0167 a Share
(06:08 GMT) *Cellnex 1Q-End Net Debt EUR16.8B
(06:08 GMT) *Cellnex: No Anticipated Impact From Tariffs
(06:25 GMT) Cellnex Net Loss Widens on Layoff Costs

By Najat Kantouar

Cellnex Telecom reported a widened net loss for the first quarter due to costs from job cuts but confirmed its outlook for the year, saying it anticipates no impact from tariffs.

The Spanish telecommunications-infrastructure company reported Friday a net loss of 49 million euros ($55 million) compared with a loss of 39 million euros for the same period a year earlier. This compared with consensus expectations of a 26.5 million euros loss based on five analysts' estimates taken from FactSet.

The company attributed the result to the impact of a provision relating to a redundancy plan in Spain for 209 people.

Adjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization increased 2.6% to 798 million euros, while the corresponding margin expanded to 83% from 82%.

Revenue rose to 964 million euros from 946 million euros, below consensus expectations of 985.7 million euros.

Cellnex reiterated its full-year guidance and said tariffs won't hurt the company.

The company said it has completed 93% of a 800 million-euro buyback program launched in March.

Write to Najat Kantouar at najat.kantouar@wsj.com

data.title: Yageo Boosts Bid for Shibaura Electronics
data.description:
Yageo will increase its tender offer price for Shibaura Electronics to 6,200 yen per share, according to a Thursday Taiwan Exchange filing.

The acquisition will begin on Friday, May 9.

The deal, led by YAGEO Electronics Japan, aims to expand Yageo's sensor business and global reach,

The electronics company will fund the deal through cash and debt, it added.

data.title: Market Chatter: Northern Star Resources' Underperformance to Reverse in Fiscal Q4, Says Citi, The Australian Reports
data.description:
Northern Star Resources' underperformance will be expectedly reversed in the fiscal fourth quarter once its 2026 guidance is downgraded and Gold Road Resources sells its 3.4% stake in the company, said Citi Analyst Kate McCutcheon, according to a Friday The Australian report.

Citi upgraded Northern Star Resources' rating from neutral to buy and increased its price target by 13% to AU$22.50.

Disclaimer

Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.

data.title: U.S. Treasury Yields Stabilize After Thursday's Rise — Market Talk
data.description: U.S. Treasury yields are little changed in early European trade after they rose across maturities following the U.S.-U.K. trade agreement on Thursday. "Well, it's not actually a deal, instead being an 'Agreement in Principle' between the U.K. and U.S., though at least it's finally some sort of concrete progress on the trade front," says Pepperstone senior research strategist Michael Brown in a note. The two-year Treasury yield falls 2.5 basis points to last trade at 3.868%; while the 10- and 30-year Treasury yields are largely flat at 4.366% and 4.828%, respectively, according to LSEG data. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0620 GMT - U.S. Treasury yields are little changed in early European trade after they rose across maturities following the U.S.-U.K. trade agreement on Thursday. "Well, it's not actually a deal, instead being an 'Agreement in Principle' between the U.K. and U.S., though at least it's finally some sort of concrete progress on the trade front," says Pepperstone senior research strategist Michael Brown in a note. The two-year Treasury yield falls 2.5 basis points to last trade at 3.868%; while the 10- and 30-year Treasury yields are largely flat at 4.366% and 4.828%, respectively, according to LSEG data. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0609 GMT - Europe's automakers shouldn't get too excited by the tariff carve-out offered for U.K. cars, ING economists caution. Washington and London have reached a framework trade deal that would include an exemption for U.K. cars from a 25% levy imposed on the sector as part of President Trump's package of sweeping import tariffs. Still, most of the British cars sold in America are premium brands like Jaguar-Land Rover, Mini, or Rolls-Royce. That makes the deal tricky to replicate for European autos, which compete more directly with U.S. producers, ING says. "Where deals are done, the import quotas may be less generous than the one agreed with the U.K.," the Dutch bank says.(joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby))

0608 GMT - Italian government bonds, or BTPs, remain the top pick in the eurozone sovereign space for Societe Generale Research's rates strategists. BTPs, alongside French government bonds, or OATs, look 10-20 basis points cheaper than fair value, say Ninon Bachet and Sean Kou in a note. "We still prefer long positions in 10-year BTP-Bund," as the spread continued to show relative strength amid the tariff scare, they say. Meanwhile, the political uncertainty around France in the next few months and the risk of negative fiscal headlines, justifies the cheapness in 10-year OAT-Bund spreads, they say. The 10-year BTP-Bund yield spread closed at 105 basis points on Thursday; the 10-year OAT-Bund yield spread ended at 70.5 basis points, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0550 GMT - A trade deal with the U.S. won't stymie Britain's efforts to get closer to the EU, economists at Dutch bank ING write in an investor note. Washington and London reached a framework agreement for a trade deal that would reduce American tariffs on British goods in exchange for the U.K. buying more U.S. goods. But the concessions offered by London aren't that major, ING says. And crucially, the country isn't budging on its food standards, which bar the import of many American products, meaning the deal won't jeopardise British efforts to build closer trade ties with its EU peers across the English Channel, the economists say. "Securing closer access is a key priority for the U.K. government, as it tries to rebuild the public finances," they say. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0545 GMT - Defense issuance by European Union member countries is unlikely to impact government bond issuance meaningfully this year, Societe Generale Research's Ninon Bachet and Sean Kou say in a note. "We believe most are unlikely to fully utilize the 1.5% of GDP allowance," the rates strategists say. Sixteen member states have submitted a request to the European Commission to activate the national escape clause. "We lack sufficient insight into the intentions of these countries to make reliable supply forecasts, but we expect a limited impact on 2025 bond issuance, with more activity anticipated in 2026," the strategists say. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0541 GMT - The damage caused by U.S. tariffs has yet to show up in China's trade data, Zhiwei Zhang, president and chief economist at Pinpoint Asset Management, says after April figures showed surprising resilience in exports. Zhang says the print could be partly due to trans-shipment of goods via other countries, and partly because of trade contracts signed before the tariffs were announced. He expects future trade data to gradually weaken over the next few months. If trade negotiations between China and the U.S. yield an agreement soon that brings down tariffs, that could mitigate the shock to global trade, Zhang writes in a note. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

0539 GMT - A trade deal with the U.S. won't act as a big spur for growth in the U.K. economy, Capital Economics' Paul Dales says. President Donald Trump said Thursday that his administration had reached a deal with the U.K. on a framework with an agreement that would include cutting tariffs on U.K. steel and cars in exchange for Britain buying Boeing products and allowing more access to the country for American farmers. But the deal is limited and in any case, Britain doesn't stand to suffer much compared with peer economies from Trump's tariffs, Dales says. "It follows that trimming some of those tariffs won't reduce that small drag by much," he says. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0530 GMT - U.S. Treasury yields are expected to move moderately higher even as they face opposing forces, SEB Research's Jussi Hiljanen says in a note. These include anticipated policy rate cuts versus fiscal considerations, erosion of trust in U.S. policy, unattractive valuations and rotation into European bonds, the chief rates strategist says. SEB Research expects the 10-year Treasury yield to trade near 4.50% in coming months, slightly above current levels, and to increase to 4.80% in 2026. The 10-year Treasury yield, which rose 10 basis points on Thursday, last traded about 1 basis point lower at 4.365%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0526 GMT - The 10-year German Bund yield is expected to remain around 2.50% in the coming months, in line with current levels, says SEB Research's Jussi Hiljanen in a note. "No meaningful decline [in yields] on the horizon," the chief rates strategist says. After a selloff in German government bonds in March triggered by Germany's fiscal plans, eurozone government bonds rallied amid trade tensions and expectations of more European Central Bank rate cuts, he says. "Support from anticipated ECB rate cuts and rotation to eurozone government bonds are balanced by high financing needs and the ongoing Eurosystem balance sheet reduction." On Thursday, the 10-year Bund yield closed at 2.530%, up 1.6 basis points, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0516 GMT - Japan's real wages will likely keep falling in the next few months due to persistent inflation, SMBC Nikko Securities economists say. Government data released Friday showed that wages adjusted for consumer prices excluding imputed rent fell 2.1% in March from a year earlier, vsFebruary's 1.5% drop. The government has begun releasing figures for wages adjusted for overall consumer prices to make international comparisons easier. The common global measure showed a 1.5% on year fall in March. "As food price growth eases and the effects of base pay increases become apparent, real wages are likely to turn positive," SMBC Nikko economists say. They expect the new measure to start rising in the summer.(megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

RNS Number : 9665H Picton Property Income Limited 09 May 2025  

9 May 2025

PICTON PROPERTY INCOME LIMITED

('Picton' or the 'Company')

Transaction in Own Shares

Picton Property Income Limited announces it has purchased for cancellation the following number of its Ordinary Shares through Stifel Nicolaus Europe Limited:

Ordinary Shares:

PICTON PROPERTY INCOME LIMITED

8 May 2025

75.7

Highest price per share (p):

76.7

76.3

Following this transaction, the Company has 531,689,205 Ordinary Shares in issue.

This number represents the total voting rights in the Company and may be used by shareholders as the denominator for the calculations by which they can determine if they are required to notify their interest in, or a change to their interest in, the Company under the Financial Conduct Authority's Disclosure Guidance and Transparency Rules.

For further information:

Picton

Kathy Thompson, Company Secretary

020 7011 9988, kathy.thompson@picton.co.uk

About Picton

Established in 2005, Picton is listed on the main market of the London Stock Exchange and is a constituent of a number of EPRA indices including the FTSE EPRA Nareit Global Index.

Picton owns and actively manages a £737 million UK commercial property portfolio, invested across 48 assets and with around 350 occupiers (as at 31 December 2024).

Through an occupier focused, opportunity led approach, Picton aims to be one of the consistently best performing diversified UK REITs and has delivered upper quartile outperformance and a consistently higher income return than the MSCI Quarterly Property Index since launch. 

With a portfolio strategically positioned to capture income and capital growth, currently weighted towards the industrial sector, Picton's agile business model provides flexibility to adapt to evolving market trends over the long-term.

Picton has a responsible approach to business and is committed to being net zero carbon by 2040.

For more information please visit:www.picton.co.uk

data.title: Abu Dhabi's IHC partners with BlackRock to launch $1 billion reinsurance venture
data.description:
Abu Dhabi's largest listed company IHC said on Friday it plans to set up a new $1 billion artificial intelligence-driven reinsurance platform with BlackRock.

The yet to be named new venture will target $10 billion in liabilities, with BlackRock and Abu Dhabi alternative investment firm Lunate joining as partners, IHC said in a statement.

BlackRock will provide a minority investment commitment to the venture and provide insurance asset management advisory and technology capabilities, it added.

Lunate will offer expertise in private and public markets and its global, multi-asset investment experience in supporting the platform, it added. The platform will be headquartered in Abu Dhabi's financial centre ADGM.

IHC said the new company will be chaired by Sultan Al Jaber, the United Arab Emirates' minister of industry and advanced technology, and led by Mark Wilson, the former chief executive of Aviva and AIA Group.

data.title: British Property Portal Rightmove Affirms FY25 Outlook
data.description:
Rightmove affirmed Friday its expectations for full-year 2025 as part of its trading update for the four months ended April 30.

The British property portal maintained its guidance of 8% to 10% revenue growth in the year.

data.title: Essity appoints Ulrika Kolsrud as new CEO
data.description: Sweden's Essity has appointed Ulrika Kolsrud as its new chief executive, the hygiene products maker said on Friday.

data.title: Malaysia's Construction Sector Growth Eases to 16.6% in Q1
data.description:
The value of work done in Malaysia's construction sector rose 16.6% in the first quarter, softer than the 23.1% growth a month prior, according to data released by the country's Department of Statistics on Friday.

The performance is attributable to a 35.5% expansion in the special trade activities and a 27% growth in residential buildings sub-sectors.

Meanwhile, the non-residential buildings sub-sector expanded 21%, while the civil engineering sub-sector rose 3.7%, the data showed.

data.title: SIG's top boss Slark to resign
data.description: British building materials supplier SIG said on Friday its CEO Gavin Slark will resign before the end of this year to take up a new role at peer Travis Perkins .

data.title: Travis Perkins: Slark to Become CEO No Later Than Jan 1
